speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead and read it. Oh, you can't read it. Can you--

speaker: interviewer
text: I can read this. I was going to read this part. Or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. My name is Mary Kasdan. Today's date is September 22, 1995. Conducting an interview with survivor Maurice Moore, who born Miodownik. The interview is being conducted in Cypress, California, USA, in English. My name Mary Kasdan. I'm conducting an interview with Maurice Moore, here in Cypress, California, USA, on September 22. Go ahead? OK. Would you tell us your name at birth, and the spelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Maurice Moore. And I was born in Warsaw July 15, 1923.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell Maurice and Moore, so we get it right?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-A-U-R-I-C-E M-O-O-R-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What was your name at birth, and would you spell it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name in Poland was Moniek Miodownik. That would be spelled M-O-N-I-E-K M-I-O-D-O-W-N-I-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were born in Warsaw, Poland, and your age now is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I'm 72 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about the slight confusion about your age?

speaker: interviewee
text: The slight confusion? I have no idea. I-- for some reason-- I don't know if I am right of-- or the records of the camp that I received just a few months ago, while I was there in-- in Auschwitz. To be honest with you, after the war I just ideally said, I was born July 15, 1923, and I kept it on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did the records from the camp say?

speaker: interviewee
text: The record says that I was born in August of 1925. I really-- I really don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Could you tell us a little bit about Warsaw when you were a child? Your view of it, how big it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember when I was a very little child, we used to live in Warsaw in a non-Jewish neighborhood. When I reached school age we-- my father and mother sent me to a religious school-- a religious day school-- where we learned half a day English and half a day Hebrew. By 1936, the conditions-- political conditions-- and anti-Semitism was so pronounced. And it was almost dangerous to live in a non-Jewish neighborhood. And we moved into a Jewish neighborhood, next to where my grandmother lived-- on Lewki 34.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, let's go back. Let's see, 1936, and you were born in '23. So you would have been 13 at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember that age.

speaker: interviewer
text: About 13. 11 to 13. But--

speaker: interviewee
text: Most likely, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before that. What was it like going to school? Was there a lot of anti-Semitism at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, every morning we used to fight. The Polish boys used to tell me that the Jewish boys killed their god. And that's what the priest told them. And as a result they were angry, although we grew up together and we were friends. And all of a sudden things changed. But they started going to school. And we got stopped going to school. Things changed entirely. And we were no more close friends, although we were at times friends. So we fought a little and we were friends a little. And that's the time-- went by that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the beginning of school?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the school, yeah. But when I was like seven, eight, nine, 10 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say you were friends at times, you mean you played together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were playing football. You know, this was-- soccer was the big thing. Every boy in Poland used to play soccer.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me a little bit about the school that you went to.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was a child?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you first started school.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a real wonderful school. All the children were Jewish and the teachers were mixed. And the Polish sessions we had Polish teachers, and-- and the Hebrew teachers, natural Jewish rabbis. It wasn't like a modern Hebrew school that we know of now. It was a Torah learning session-- how to read and interpret and learn Hebrew, [YIDDISH], how to-- how to pray, and the laws, and so forth. That was the Hebrew education that we received.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these rabbis on the faculty of the school--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or did they come in?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are faculty rabbis. That was their profession. They were professional teachers. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What languages did you speak at home and at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home we spoke Yiddish and Polish. But my parents spoke Russian. Because my mother went to school when the Russians occupied Poland. So she was educated in Polish and Russian, and French and Yiddish and Hebrew. So they were quite educated people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit more about that. How she went to school, you said, when the Russians occupied Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Both of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Tell me just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the end of the First World War, Russia was in Poland. Poland was not an independent country until 1918. And my mother was born in 1900.

speaker: interviewer
text: So her school years were during that time. So that's the reason she went to Russian schools where they also taught Polish. Now, what languages did you learn? What languages did you learn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Learn in Poland, in school? Just Yiddish, Polish, and part Hebrew. I mean, a substantial amount of Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is that what you spoke at home, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only Yiddish and Polish. And my parents spoke Russian, only when they didn't want the children to understand. But eventually we learned Russian, because it's very close to Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, now, you said your father was a very religious man. Could you tell us a little bit about that? What organizations he belonged to, what rabbi he followed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but he was a-- in-- in the jargon of Jewish view-- [NON-ENGLISH], which is a member of a certain group of people that go and support a certain rabbi. And his rabbi was from a city called Kashmir. So he was called a [NON-ENGLISH] Hasid. And that rabbi-- that Kashmir rabbi used to live a few buildings from where we lived.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were always in walking distance to that particular rabbi. And we prayed in his synagogue. And we spent holidays there, in prayer. And my father supported him just like all the other Hasidim supported this particular rabbi.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every Jew that was a Hasid in Warsaw had his own personal rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you say Hasid you don't mean necessarily a Hasidic Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, exactly. A Hasidic Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: An orthodox Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: An orthodox Jew. That's exactly what I mean.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to him with problems? To the rabbi with any problems?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have any problems, I was a young kid. I went to this rabbi because my father went to him, and the children usually went to shul where their father went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father go to him with problems that he might have in daily life--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not aware of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Or business? OK. Was your mother religious, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a very beautiful lady. And she was very educated, very well educated. And she was running the house. And she was socially involved. I'm not aware-- I'm not familiar what she did. But she was active in various organizations. And at that time I was too young to be aware of what the type of organizations she belonged to. Probably organizations that are also, I would say, Hasidish leaning, on the very traditional Jewish way. And almost everybody in Warsaw-- the majority of people were orthodox. So she was also the same. She was raised that way. That's the way she was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she go to the synagogue often, like your father did?

speaker: interviewee
text: She went to the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it actually a synagogue that the rabbi had, or was in his house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it wasn't a synagogue. It was like a house. And the living room was a room that we did the prayers. It was a larger living room and that's-- like a private shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father belong to any other organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think he was a member of a Orthodox political party, which is called Agudath Israel. And it's a very well-known organization. It's still in existence today.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you? Were you in any organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not. I didn't belong to--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You went to school until you were how old? How old were you when you left school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd say about 14.

speaker: interviewer
text: 14. And what did you do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: While I was going to school I worked part-time in a-- a printing and rubber stamp company that was right-- our neighbors had the-- and I was working there. I was delivering things. I was doing-- go kind of chores. And they used to give me $5 a week for part-time work. And I-- it was handy money, and naturally I gave it to my mother because I didn't need any money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father's work?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father-- I'll tell you-- most of the time he wasn't home. He was in the fields. His work required him to be outside the home. He was buying fruits of the trees by ranchers. And when the fruit ripens-- and he had people watching the ranches-- when the fruit ripens he organize it to bring it to Warsaw and sell it to wholesalers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the reason he did that is he was a [YIDDISH] person and he needed to do something that will allow him to be a [YIDDISH]. So then on Fridays you also stopped working? Stopped doing whatever you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friday was a busy day home. My mother used to bake challahs, make gefilte fish. Everything was made from scratch. It was like a holiday.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was enjoyable? Fond memories? Do you have fond memories of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, very, very important. Everything was made home. The cakes, the fish, the challah, the chicken-- whatever she made was home-cooked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember having any goals or dreams as a young person, when you were working, say, in the print shop? After you left school at 14 what did you think was ahead of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'll tell you what. The situation in Poland was so that almost every Jewish young man, being aware of situation that we lived at the time, was-- our dream was to go either to Palestine, at the time, or to the United States. Almost everyone had some member of his family in America. And we had very good news that in America you can live a freer life. A life of equality. And you don't have to worry about being Jewish, or being beaten up if you go in a neighborhood other than a Jewish neighborhood. And, the-- also the economic situation in Poland was not exactly the best. So Jewish boys usually dreamed of going to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that included me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family ever talk about possibly leaving, maybe when--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my father had a cousin in America. So that gave us a little bit of an idea-- in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about when you started to hear about trouble in Germany? Didn't they think of leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew about trouble in Germany when I was 10 years old. And there were always things happen to Germany, because a lot of Jewish people from Poland used to emigrate to Germany in order to get jobs there, or be in business there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we know exactly-- and Germany bordered Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So when these various conditions happened in Germany we were very well aware what's happening in Germany. To the Jewish people, especially. When did it start affecting your life? Was it as early as 1933, or later?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened in '35?

speaker: interviewee
text: We moved to the Jewish neighborhoods.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because the anti-Semitism was bad?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you voluntarily move then to the Jewish neighborhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this the ghetto, yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was just the Jewish neighborhood, just like here would be Fairfax.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you go from one apartment to another? Is that how it worked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We just left one apartment. In Poland, you used to buy apartments. You used to sell an apartment and buy an apartment. It wasn't really your property but it was like rent control, you know. Like, if you pay-- let's see-- $200 a month, whoever you sold them to also paid $200 a month.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had a reason why to pay key money, they used to call it. I remember that. How was the rise of Nazism perceived in your family and community? What did you think was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, most of the people that we knew, we were praying for Mashiyah to come. But, practically, people were really concerned. And the Polacks, they acted as if they would be the Germans. So we were afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say they acted as if they would be the Germans--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because when a Jewish person-- especially when he was bearded and he had the outfit of a religious Jew-- went in a non-Jewish neighborhood, he was attacked. His beard was shorn off. He was beaten up by hooligans. I mean, not 100% of the Polish population acted that way, but a great-- a lot of people were anti-Semitic.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did all the Jewish businesses move into these Jewish neighborhoods, then? People took their business--

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish businesses-- now, the business owners [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] weren't-- they were the owners of the businesses, but the people that worked for them were non-Jews. They run the businesses in the non-Jewish neighborhoods.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it wasn't a direct one-on-one relationship, where a Hasidic Jew was in front of a business in a non-Jewish neighborhood. There was-- non-Jewish people were running the business.

speaker: interviewee
text: And certain-- and not all the Jews in Poland-- in Warsaw-- were Hasidim. They were very much Westernized. The business people were a different group of people, especially people in the retail business, people in banking, and insurances. They were entirely different. A different group of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: They wore modern Western clothing. They were shaved. They wore shirt and ties. They were just like Americans now. There was the-- they were a different class of people. Not everybody was Hasidish, or wore a beard in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they were still the targets of anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: They couldn't be recognized so easily. A man-- a Jewish person that had a beard, and he had these dark black clothes on-- he was easily identified. So he was beaten up. It wasn't like a-- a revolt against the Jews, but almost every day some Jewish people in Warsaw in certain neighborhoods were attacked because they were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you dress like? Your father dressed in the old--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Western. I-- I didn't want that. I was dressed like now.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about September 1, 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: September 1, 1939, was the first day the Germans attacked Poland. And planes were over Warsaw with scattered bombardment, not very heavy. And there was the mobilization right away. And Germany started marching into Poland. And they marched all over Poland in, maybe, eight days. They were on the outskirts of the city of Warsaw. And then the military decided that they will defend the city of Warsaw. And the German encircled the city of Warsaw with artillery, and were shooting down into the city. And they were shooting down into the city. That was the third week of September in 1939. And that where my tragedy happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you tell us about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, there was an artillery shell, fell into the building that we lived in on the first floor, sending down the floor completely into the basement. And all my family, and many other neighbors, were in the basement as a-- in the-- at night, because they told us to go into the basement because they were bombarding it from airplanes. But the bombardment was not from airplanes. They were from artillery. And they sent down the floor, and as a result my parents, and one my brothers, plus-- including many members of my family, totalling 17 people of my family-- got killed in that particular basement. But my grandmother that was also in that basement was not killed. And the reason was-- she was in a pocket where she could get air. The most of the people died because of lack of air. They suffocated. And my brother and I were in the third floor of the building, that where we lived. And we didn't even know that because there was no explosion.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we got down early in the morning, about 6 o'clock, the neighbors that were not affected did not want to believe that we were not with our parents, and told us what happened. And we were in charge of digging out the people from the basement. And we were putting them all on the-- on the yard-- on the courtyard of the building.

speaker: interviewee
text: And two of my uncles survived, my mother's two brothers. And my brother and I. But four men. And each member of the family had to bury their own members of the family that got killed in that particular basement. And it happens-- that happens in many basements in the city of Warsaw. Not only in the Jewish neighborhoods but in the non-Jewish neighborhoods, as well. So they weren't killed because they were Jews, it's just a unfortunate-- fortune of wars.

speaker: interviewer
text: The artillery was not flying? It was on hillsides around Warsaw when they sent these shells in? Was it on hillsides or was it in the air?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the artillery came this way. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it from planes, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Artillery-- all around Warsaw, there was-- encircled the city of Warsaw-- and the artillery were shooting down into the city just everywhere. And we had to bury our own dead, because at that time nobody wanted to accept Polish money. Even if you had the money you couldn't get anybody to work for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had to take our own cadavers-- members-- and put them on pushcarts. And taking the pushcart to the Warsaw cemetery-- Jewish cemetery-- digging a common grave. And we dug a grave for 37 people from our building.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we buried them according to the Jewish law, as a group in their clothes. And that was the first experience that I had in the war of a personal nature.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what went through your mind when you were burying your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were frozen. My mind wasn't functioning. I had just to do things because this was the things to do. There was no past. There was no future. There was just the present. What do we do now? After we buried our dead we were sitting shiva according to the Jewish customs. And the whole building was sitting shiva. And it was a tragedy, enormous proportions. We lost our most important people. And my-- thank God, Grandma was alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she took care of us, my brother and I. And she cooked for us, she baked for us. She took-- she was a mother all over again. And this is what happened in September of 1939. Did the rabbi that your father followed survive that bombardment? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I personally didn't meet him after that day. I did not go there. I was very much hurt because we always were thinking that good people that do good deeds and good things should happen to them. And I wouldn't consider that as a good thing that happened to me. I really almost denounced the religiosity. I wasn't [NON-ENGLISH] any more. I mean, I was observing because of my father. But we had to say Kaddish every day because that-- that was the custom. So we had to daven and say Kaddish for 11 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were no rabbis at the mass burial?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't need a rabbi. Everybody-- every Jew can conduct services.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next, to you? You're living with your grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: During that time there was a question of, we have to eat. Somebody has to support us. And there was no-- money ran out, whatever little money we had. And all the things-- like food and whatever people need for everyday survival, got more expensive. Inflation sets in. And the woman that owned the printing and rubber stamp company offered me a partnership.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was your neighbor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I knew how to make rubber stamps. And people used to come from all over to buy rubber stamps of every description. And so I started making money because we started charging prices-- high prices-- because we couldn't replace the raw materials. They weren't available.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is what made me money to survive in the ghetto-- and right after the tragedy that happened.
